Title: KTM rider killed after crash with car in Kemaman
Article:
KTM rider killed after crash with car in Kemaman
KEMAMAN: A man was killed after the motorcycle he was riding crashed into a car at Km 158 of the Kuala Terengganu-Kuantan road near here yesterday evening.
In the incident at 4.20pm, Zulkarnain Rawi, 51, who was riding a KTM 250 Duke motorcycle, died at the scene.
District police chief Mohd Razi Rosli said the victim was from Kampung Chendor Baru, Kuantan, Pahang, and worked as a lorry driver.
He said the victim was travelling from Bandar Chukai towards Chendor Baru at the time of the incident.
"The accident is believed to have occurred when the victim's motorcycle collided with a Perodua Myvi driven by an elderly man who was travelling from the Kidney Dialysis Centre towards Bandar Chukai.
"Upon reaching the traffic light junction there, the victim is said to have continued riding while the traffic light was flashing green.
"At the same time, the Perodua Myvi emerged from the junction and the victim failed to avoid it before crashing into the car," he said.
Razi said the victim was pronounced dead at the scene due to severe head and bodily injuries.
"The victim's body was later sent to the Forensic Unit of Kemaman Hospital for a post-mortem, while the 78-year-old car driver, who sustained minor injuries, was treated at the same hospital," he said.
The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987, which carries a jail term of between five and 10 years, a fine of up to RM50,000, and disqualification from holding a driving licence for at least five years upon conviction.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Geopolitical risks, oil price surge to keep KLCI range-bound, says HLIB
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Heightened geopolitical tensions and rising oil prices, driven by the ongoing US-Iran impasse, are expected to keep the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI index in a range-bound consolidation phase after gaining 28.5 points month-to-date, according to Hong Leong Investment Bank (HLIB).
The research house said market sentiment is likely to remain cautious and headline-driven after US President Donald Trump described the ceasefire as "on life support" amid renewed Gulf tensions and continued Israel-Lebanon border clashes.
Attention now turns to the high-stakes Trump–Xi summit in Beijing, alongside the ongoing first quarter of 2025 (Q1 FY26) earnings season. Investors will be closely watching corporate guidance as they weigh still-resilient fundamentals against rising geopolitical risks, elevated energy prices, and emerging growth headwinds," HLIB said in a note.
From a technical perspective, HLIB said the KLCI remains in an uptrend after rebounding 6.25 per cent from its year-to-date low of 1,664 on March 9 to an intraday high of 1,768 on May 7, although recent gains are being consolidated following a shooting star formation on May 7.
The benchmark index continues to trade above its rising trendline, supported by a MA20-MA50 golden cross.
HLIB said maintaining above the 1,730 level, which represents the 76.4 per cent Fibonacci retracement, is crucial to sustaining bullish momentum, with upside targets at 1,768, 1,789 and 1,800.
However, a breach below 1,730 could trigger deeper consolidation towards 1,723, 1,710 and possibly the 1,700 psychological level. Weekly support is pegged at 1,710-1,730, while resistance stands at 1,768-1,780.
Despite weaker Wall Street and regional market sentiment, the KLCI edged up 5.3 points to 1,750.6 on selective bargain hunting in blue-chip counters such as Press Metal Aluminium Holdings Bhd, Petronas Chemicals Group Bhd, IHH Healthcare Bhd, Malayan Banking Bhd, Tenaga Nasional Bhd and Telekom Malaysia Bhd after declines over the previous two trading sessions.
Market breadth remained steady at 1.01, while trading volume rose 17.1 per cent to 4.92 billion shares valued at RM3.60 billion.
Foreign institutions remained net sellers at RM65 million, alongside local retailers at RM9 million, while local institutions turned net buyers with RM74 million in purchases.
Meanwhile, Asian markets closed mixed as the AI-driven chip rally lost momentum, with investors turning cautious ahead of US CPI data and persistent US–Iran tensions.
HLIB said tighter financial conditions driven by elevated oil (around US$105 Brent), rising bond yields, and a stronger dollar are increasingly weighing on risk appetite and global growth prospects.
"Geopolitical risks also escalated after Trump rejected Iran's latest peace proposal, while heightened military activity around the Strait of Hormuz cast doubt on an already fragile ceasefire. Still, losses were partly cushioned by cautious optimism ahead of the Trump–Xi meeting in Beijing, with hopes for progress on tariffs, Taiwan, and a potential extension of the October 2025 trade truce," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fugitive Malaysian financier Jho Low seeks Trump pardon, WSJ reports
Article:
WASHINGTON: Malaysian financier Low Taek Jho, widely known as Jho Low and now a fugitive, has sought a pardon from US President Donald Trump, the Wall Street Journal reported on Tuesday.
Reuters could not immediately verify the report.
The request was filed in recent weeks, the Journal said, citing people familiar with the matter, and if granted would remove US criminal charges against him.
A Justice Department website lists a pending request for a "Pardon after Completion of Sentence" under Taek Jho Low that was filed this year, the report said.
A White House official said Low's request was not currently on the White House's radar, WSJ added. - Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Better employee well-being leads to higher productivity, lower burnout
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: After struggling with mounting workloads and work-related anxiety for two years, former creative writer and marketing executive Clarissa (not her real name) finally tendered her resignation last month.
The 29-year-old said she had endured emotional exhaustion and burnout after being overwhelmed by work demands, which gradually affected her mental well-being and personal life.
Almost every night, she would wake up abruptly, anxious about what awaited her at work the next day, particularly the pressure from her supervisor at the publishing company where she worked.
Clarissa, who joined the company in 2022, said she initially enjoyed her job and was passionate about her work, but the situation changed after a Work-From-Home (WFH) policy was introduced in 2024.
"At first, I was very happy when I was allowed to work from home two to three times a week. But I found my workload increasing significantly over time.
"More and more tasks were given to me because I was considered efficient, to the point that I frequently had to sacrifice my days off to complete assignments and respond to office messages.
"Eventually, people began assigning me duties that were not even related to my actual job scope," she told Bernama.
She said the constant criticism over minor mistakes, coupled with the expectation to remain available even during leave days, left her emotionally and physically drained.
Now searching for a new job, Clarissa hopes her future employer will place greater emphasis on employee well-being and work-life balance.
In another case, Minah (not her real name), a 33-year-old former human resource executive, said she endured mental distress as her employer publicly humiliated her in front of her colleagues, even over minor mistakes.
"This may be common in Asian workplace culture, but it is unacceptable because it does not solve the problem.
"Instead, it affects employees' mental health and makes them unhappy in their workplace.
"Every job requires teamwork, and it is important for employees to have a good relationship with their superiors. That is why I decided to resign to protect my mental health," she said, adding that she has a new job now with flexible working hours.
Burnout
Many employees experience work-related stress and burnout due to excessive workloads, long working hours, unrealistic expectations and continuous pressure to meet deadlines.
Studies done in Malaysia show an increase in workers reporting burnout.
According to HR platform Employment Hero's Wellness at Work Report, 67 per cent of workers reported experiencing burnout in 2024, increasing from 58 per cent in 2022.
Jobstreet by SEEK's Workplace Happiness Index 2026 report, based on 2025 data, shows that although seven in 10 Malaysian workers were happy at work, stress and burnout remained persistent concerns.
The findings indicate that workplace happiness is often driven by fundamentals such as work environment, colleagues and daily responsibilities.
The report, launched last month, was based on responses from about 1,000 individuals aged 18 to 64.
Forty-one per cent of respondents reported feeling burnt out or exhausted; and while 68 per cent found their jobs fulfilling, only 36 per cent were satisfied with their stress levels.
Three in 10 workers remained either unhappy or neutral about their jobs.
Among those who were unhappy, 71 per cent said a better work-life balance would improve their well-being, second only to higher pay, highlighting the impact of unmanaged stress on productivity and long-term outcomes.
Health issues
Meanwhile, Sunway Medical Centre resident medical officer and certified lifestyle medicine physician Dr Wee Hui Yin said the emphasis on productivity in the workplace has led to more people developing chronic stress and burnout, which increase their chances of developing non-communicable diseases, including diabetes, heart disease and hypertension.
She said medical check-ups often fail to detect many of the health issues caused by stress, but the patients concerned frequently show signs of stress during their medical consultations and history evaluation sessions.
"Many of them don't call it burnout, but it appears as fatigue, irritability, sleep issues, and lower tolerance at work and home," she told Bernama in a written interview.
Dr Wee said these patients can also experience psychological distress where they develop depression, anxiety, sleep disturbances and dangerous drinking habits, which can ultimately create additional health threats for them.
"Stress also causes inflammation and stiffening of the arteries, which helps explain why some people develop heart problems even without obvious risk factors," she said.
She added that company health screening programmes will not achieve meaningful results if they focus only on annual tests, without appropriate post-screening medical follow-up and lifestyle interventions.
"Some patients come every year, but their results remain the same. Often, it is not due to lack of awareness, but a lack of time, guidance and support, accompanied by long working hours, poor routines and the feeling of not knowing where to start," she added.
She said to improve employees' physical and mental well-being, employers should offer practical support like childcare services, clear work boundaries and self-care to reduce their stress levels.
Flexibility
Commenting on employee well-being, cloud-based business application platform Kintone's Southeast Asia managing director Tsubasa Nakazawa said leaders (managers and supervisors) play a crucial role in reducing workplace friction by ensuring their teams have clear priorities, accessible information and transparent workflows.
"Usually they (leaders) don't leave their teams guessing, and they will create clearer priorities and ensure work can move forward without employees having to constantly chase visibility or rely on informal access to managers," he told Bernama in an email interview.
Nakazawa said organisations that establish clear ownership, transparent project visibility and proper documentation tend to create a more stable and productive work environment.
"When teams have shared visibility over projects and decisions are properly documented, employees spend less energy trying to prove they are working and more energy actually moving the work forward," he said.
He added that in workplaces with flexible working arrangements, office time should also become more purposeful, focusing on mentoring, collaboration and problem-solving rather than serving as the default location for all tasks.
"In workplaces where flexibility functions smoothly, people understand what they are accountable for, how decisions are made, where information sits and how collaboration happens.
"The leaders who make it work well are those who create structure, clarity and trust in ways that allow flexibility to support performance rather than compete with it," he added.
Jobstreet by SEEK managing director Nicholas Lam said to strengthen employee well-being, employers should clearly connect their (employees') daily responsibilities to the organisation's broader mission and values, helping them see how their work makes a meaningful impact.
"Employers should foster (work) environments that help reduce stress and burnout, where employees feel valued, motivated and supported to do their best work," he said in a statement to Bernama, adding that when employers invest in purpose-driven roles and supportive cultures, and listen to the diverse needs of their workforce, the impact is tangible, translating to higher engagement, productivity and retention.
Co-founder and chief executive officer of co-working space provider Infinity8 Lee Sheah Liang said that as workplace burnout becomes increasingly common, many employees are now seeking more conducive work environments that offer flexibility, purpose and breathing space, instead of the rigid structure associated with traditional offices.
(A co-working space is a shared, flexible office environment where individuals from different companies, freelancers, entrepreneurs and remote workers work independently or collaboratively.)
Lee said co-working spaces provide employees with ready-to-use facilities, including furniture, high-speed Internet access and dedicated working areas, allowing them to simply clock in and begin work immediately.
"In co-working spaces such as Infinity8, we also provide quiet pods for those who need to focus, while employees who need to brainstorm ideas can use collaborative meeting rooms to work more efficiently," he said in an interview with Bernama.
Meanwhile, Dr Wee summed up workplace well-being by saying, "Being at work longer doesn't necessarily make employees more effective.
"Healthy employees are (ultimately) more productive, which is why seeking help promptly is important." — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Experts call for multi-dimensional income model in petrol subsidy review
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government has been urged to adopt a multi-dimensional household income classification system to ensure the proposed petrol subsidy review for high-income earners is implemented transparently and accurately.
Economist Emeritus Prof Dr Barjoyai Bardai said such an approach would provide a comprehensive assessment of financial capacity by accounting for assets, location, number of dependents, and spending patterns.
The Universiti Sains dan Teknologi Malaysia Provost and Dean of the Institute of Graduate Studies said the B40, M40, and T20 categories no longer reflect the current cost of living, particularly the disparity between urban and rural areas.
"An income of RM15,000 in Kuala Lumpur is not the same as RM15,000 in Kota Baru because living costs in major cities, towns, rural areas, and East Malaysia differ significantly," he said.
Barjoyai said a score-based multi-dimensional approach is more practical as it measures real financial capability rather than relying solely on gross monthly income.
He also cautioned the government against policies that could intensify the middle-class squeeze, where middle-income groups face mounting pressure from taxes and subsidy reductions.
On the petrol subsidy review, he suggested alternative mechanisms such as a controlled fuel price float or limiting subsidised purchases to the first 100 litres per consumer.
Meanwhile, National Professors Council fellow Azmi Hassan proposed reducing the high-income category to the top 15 per cent from the current 20 per cent, using the Main Database (Padu) as the primary reference.
He said integrating Padu data with Inland Revenue Board information would help the government identify recipients more accurately.
Azmi dismissed claims that the move would be unfair to high-income earners, stressing the proposal only involved households with strong financial standing.
Federation of Malaysian Consumers Associations chief executive officer Dr T. Saravanan said public concerns remain over data accuracy involving income levels, employment status, and family dependents.
"If the data is inaccurate, there is a risk of exclusion or mistargeting. Before full implementation, the government must ensure review and appeal processes are simple and accessible," he said.
Saravanan also proposed incentives such as tax relief or additional targeted assistance if subsidy reductions are implemented for certain groups.
He said the government should continue prioritising efforts to curb fuel smuggling and improve the efficiency of distribution systems.
Consumer-Friendly Organisation of Malaysia deputy president Azlin Othman said the review is a reasonable step toward ensuring aid is targeted and sustainable while strengthening the country's fiscal position.
However, she stressed that rationalisation measures must be implemented in stages and accompanied by clear communication to avoid negative public perception.
On Sunday, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said the government was still studying the best mechanism for the petrol subsidy review, and a decision would be announced soon.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US processes US$35.5bil in refunds for tariffs imposed under IEEPA
Article:
ISTANBUL: US authorities have processed about US$35.5 billion in refunds and interest for tariffs imposed by President Donald Trump under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), which the Supreme Court later ruled did not authorise the tariffs.
In a filing with the US Court of International Trade, US Customs and Border Protection said Tuesday that the first phase of its tariff refund system was opened for use on April 20, Anadolu Ajansi reported.
As of May 11, a total of 126,237 claims had been submitted, of which 86,874 passed validation checks, the agency said.
The validated claims covered 15.1 million import entries, while more than 8.3 million of them were liquidated or reliquidated after IEEPA duties were removed, it added.
The estimated amount of refunds and interest for the reprocessed entries stood at about US$35.5 billion as of May 11, according to the filing.
The US Supreme Court ruled on Feb 20 that IEEPA, the law cited by Trump to impose the tariffs, did not authorise the president to levy such duties.
Following the decision, thousands of companies sued the US government to seek refunds for tariffs they had paid
Some companies have begun receiving refunds for the IEEPA tariffs as of Tuesday, according to media reports.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump says he does not consider economic impacts on Americans during Iran talks
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Tuesday that he does not take into consideration the economic toll being borne by Americans when he engages with Iran over negotiations to end the war he started in February, Anadolu Ajansi reported
"Not even a little bit," Trump said in response to a reporter's question regarding to what extent he considers Americans' financial situations during the talks. "The only thing that matters when I am talking about Iran: they can't have a nuclear weapon. I don't think about Americans' financial situation. I don't think about anybody."
The US-Israeli war against Iran has cost Americans roughly US$38.1 billion in extra energy prices, according to figures compiled by the Iran War Energy Cost Tracker from Brown University's Watson School of International and Public Affairs.
The data is updating in real-time with the tally continuing to increase with each passing second. The majority of the extra costs – US$20.86 billion – have been paid for petroleum while diesel accounts for US$17.2 billion.
The average US household has paid US$290.56 more per month across both gasoline and diesel.
Regional tensions have escalated since the US and Israel launched strikes against Iran on Feb 28, triggering retaliation from Tehran against Israel as well as US allies in the Gulf, along with the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
A ceasefire took effect April 8 through Pakistani mediation, but talks in Islamabad failed to produce a lasting agreement. The truce was later extended indefinitely by Trump amid an impasse in resuming negotiations.
Trump fumed against Iran on Sunday after Tehran submitted its response to his proposal to permanently end the war, calling it "totally unacceptable."
Iranian Parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf responded, maintaining that there is "no alternative" but to accept the rights of the Iranian people outlined in Tehran's 14-point proposal.
"Any other approach will be completely inconclusive; nothing but one failure after another," Qalibaf wrote on the US social media platform X.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Walmart lays off or relocates about 1,000 corporate workers
Article:
SAN FRANCISCO: Walmart would cut or relocate about 1,000 corporate workers as it looks to combine more of its global-technology and AI product teams, the Wall Street Journal reported on Tuesday, citing people familiar with the situation.
Under new chief executive officer John Furner and a reshaped leadership team, the world's largest retailer is ramping up an AI-led digital transformation to better take on Amazon.com, Costco, and Aldi.
Head of global AI acceleration Daniel Danker, and head of global technology Suresh Kumar have reviewed the retailer's internal structures and decided to streamline some teams to operate more efficiently, the WSJ report said, citing the leaders as saying in a memo sent to staff.
Affected staff can apply for open roles within the company, the report said, citing the memo.
Many of the affected staff have been asked to relocate to Walmart's Bentonville or Northern California offices, the report said, citing the people familiar with the situation.
Walmart did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
The company has invested heavily in AI to close gaps with its largest rival, Amazon, which had a head start with its chatbot, Rufus, a Gen AI-powered shopping assistant that answers various shopping queries.
Walmart, in July last year, rolled out a suite of AI-powered "super agents" designed to improve the shopping experience for customers and streamline operations following Danker's appointment.
Furner kicked off his tenure with a cautious outlook for the coming year in February, reflecting the fragile state of US consumers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: United Airlines to resume direct flights between US, Venezuela
Article:
CARACAS, (Venezuela): US carrier United Airlines announced Tuesday that it plans to resume direct flights between the United States and Venezuela starting Aug. 11, marking its return to the route after nearly a decade, Xinhua reported.
The airline said it will operate daily nonstop flights between Houston's George Bush Intercontinental Airport and Caracas' Simon Bolivar International Airport using Boeing 737 MAX 8 aircraft.
Tickets are already on sale, with service subject to government approval.
This flight reopens a key economic gateway, creates new business opportunities and makes it easier for families to reconnect with loved ones, said United Airlines in a statement.
United Airlines' planned return follows American Airlines' resumption of direct commercial passenger flights to Venezuela on April 30, the first such US service after years of suspension.
United Airlines suspended its Venezuela service in 2017 amid political tensions between Washington and Caracas. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Wall Street slips as inflation, Iran tensions weigh
Article:
NEW YORK: The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq eased from record highs on Tuesday as hotter-than-expected inflation data and an increasingly tenuous US-Iran ceasefire prompted investors to take money off the table near the end of a robust first-quarter earnings season.
Weakness in tech shares dragged the Nasdaq down the most, while healthcare stocks, buoyed by a jump in Humana, helped limit the Dow's losses.
Despite the selloff, the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq remain close to all-time highs.
As reporting season wraps up, investors are increasingly focused on valuations, macroeconomics and geopolitical developments.
While the PHLX Semiconductor index tumbled 4.8 per cent, the index has soared 62.4 per cent this year, benefiting from the fervor about artificial intelligence.
CONSUMER PRICE RISE DISAPPOINTS
Economic data showed consumer prices rising at a faster pace than analysts anticipated as the closure of the Strait of Hormuz due to the war with Iran continued to disrupt crude supply.
"It was a slightly disappointing CPI report that showed inflation is relatively high, and it reinforces the notion that the (US Federal Reserve) is not going to be able to lower rates this year," said Oliver Pursche, senior vice president at Wealthspire Advisors, in New York.
"We've seen this multiple times this year, where it's kind of the two steps forward, one step back market cycle," Pursche added.
"Some of the chipmakers that are selling off the most, they're still up sharply year-to-date." That war, in its 11th week, showed no signs of a near-term resolution.
US President Donald Trump declared the truce was "on life support" after Tehran rejected a US proposal to end the conflict, sticking with a list of demands Trump called "garbage."
The notion of a protracted conflict raises the probability that spiking energy prices could metastasize into broader, more entrenched inflation.
That has all but squelched any hopes for an interest rate cut from the Fed this year under the presumed chairmanship of Trump nominee Kevin Warsh, whom the US Senate confirmed on Tuesday.
The odds of a rate hike are rising. Financial markets are pricing in a 31.5 per cent likelihood that the central bank will implement a 25-basis-point increase to its Fed funds target rate in December, up from 21.5 per cent on Monday, according to CME's FedWatch tool.
Trump is scheduled to travel to Beijing this week to meet Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping to address a wide array of issues, including tariffs, US military aid to Taiwan, China's potential role in brokering a peace deal with Iran, and the extension of a trade agreement regarding critical rare earth metals.
The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 4.18 points, or 0.01 per cent, to 49,700.29, the S&P 500 lost 40.25 points, or 0.54 per cent, to 7,372.82 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 360.64 points, or 1.37 per cent, to 25,913.48.
Of the 11 major sectors in the S&P 500, tech suffered the largest percentage loss, while healthcare led the gainers.
Humana advanced 5.4 per cent following Bernstein's 36 per cent price target hike. GameStop dipped 1.3 per cent following eBay's rejection of the meme stock trailblazer's US$56 billion takeover bid.
Zebra Technologies jumped 14.4 per cent after the barcode scanner maker raised its annual sales growth forecast, betting on robust demand for its products that help automate manufacturing workflows.
Hims & Hers Health tumbled 12.5 per cent after the telehealth firm missed Wall Street estimates for first-quarter revenue and posted a surprise loss.
Venture Global jumped 16.7 per cent after the LNG exporter raised its annual adjusted core profit forecast.
Declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 2.12-to-1 ratio on the NYSE. There were 116 new highs and 114 new lows on the NYSE.
On the Nasdaq, 1,493 stocks rose and 3,178 fell as declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 2.13-to-1 ratio.
The S&P 500 posted 14 new 52-week highs and 28 new lows while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 45 new highs and 140 new lows.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'The silence is unbearable': Widow of FRU crash victim shares grief
Article:
IPOH:  A year after losing her husband in the Federal Reserve Unit (FRU) crash in Teluk Intan, Haswati Rosdin still struggles  to accept that the man who once filled every corner of her life is gone.
The silence left behind by Lance Corporal Damarrulan Abd Latif, who was then 33 —  one of the nine FRU personnel killed in the crash — continues to haunt the 45-year-old widow, who said even the smallest routines now carry painful memories.
Haswati said she eventually moved out from the police quarters in December because the surroundings constantly reminded her of her late husband.
"Staying in the camp without him was too painful. Every time I saw someone wearing the uniform or saw officers marching, I was reminded of him. So I decided to move out and find another house."
Haswati described Damarrulan as a devoted husband who spent eight years caring for her and her three children, including when  she suffered a stroke in 2019.
"From the time I fell sick until I could walk again, he took care of everything. For me, he was a very kind husband. It's hard to find someone like him."
Haswati said the hardest part after his death was learning to live independently, something her husband had repeatedly reminded her to do.
"He always told me that if one day he was no longer around, I had to be strong and independent.
"Don't trouble others and don't depend too much on people.
"But when he was gone, I felt lost. We were together for eight years. Suddenly, I didn't know what to do anymore."
She recalled how her daily routines revolved around her husband, especially preparing his drinks and uniform before he left for work, even during early morning shifts.
"Now when I wake up, sometimes I still remember his favourite cup and his uniform. The things are there, but he is not. That's what hurts the most," she said.
Despite her grief, Haswati said she continued to work towards the dreams Damarrulan left behind, including buying a house for the family and ensuring their three daughters, aged between
18 and 24, succeed in their
studies.
"He always talked about wanting to buy a house for us. We used to discuss our plans together, but he did not get the chance to see it happen.
"Now, I have fulfilled that wish for him," she said at her home in Taman Klebang Jaya here.
She said her youngest daughter recently visited his grave after receiving her Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia results to show that she had passed, fulfilling a promise she had made to her late stepfather.
"Even though they were his stepchildren, he loved them like his own. He always reminded them to do their best in school."
Haswati also shared cherished memories of Damarrulan's caring nature, describing how he would take her on motorcycle rides to Cameron Highlands and around Ipoh despite his modest salary.
"He always wanted to make me happy. Even late at night, he would ask me to go around Ipoh with him.
"He once said, 'Who else will take you out one day if I'm no longer here?' At that time, I didn't understand what he meant," she said.
Haswati said she had lost  10kg after losing her husband.
"For eight years, he treated me like a queen," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'No such thing as T20-only supply chain'
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: There is a risk of "trickle-down" inflation arising from the possible removal of Budi Madani RON95 (Budi95) subsidy for the T20 group, as fuel remains a key cost component in the national supply chain, an industry body said.
However, some experts argued that removing RON95 subsidies for the T20 group is unlikely to have a direct impact on the B40 and M40 segments.
The proposal, announced by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim on Sunday, has come under scrutiny with critics saying it unfairly assumes all T20 earners are financially comfortable despite rising living costs.
There are also concerns that the move could create a cascading effect across the broader economy, potentially leading to higher costs for lower- and middle-income households through increased prices for goods and services.
SME Association of Malaysia president Dr Chin Chee Seong told Business Times that there is no such thing as a "T20-only" supply chain in the business world, noting that goods delivered by businesses owned by T20 individuals are ultimately consumed by B40 and M40 households as well.
"When service providers, sales teams and small business owners are required to pay the unsubsidised price of RM4.02 per litre, which is more than double the subsidised rate of RM1.99, operating costs will rise sharply.
"Even if the B40 and M40 groups continue to enjoy subsidised rates at the pump through Budi95, they are still likely to feel the impact through higher prices for daily goods and services as businesses adjust to these increased operating costs," he said.
Chin said the government should be careful not to create the perception that the T20 group is being repeatedly targeted, noting that many within this income bracket are also facing rising living costs and significant business expenses.
He added that instead of a sudden exclusion approach, a more practical option would be a gradual, broad-based adjustment to around RM2.20 per litre for all users.
"This would reduce the government's fiscal burden while avoiding a sharp 'cost shock' to the economy," he added.
Putra Business School economic analyst Dr Ida Md Yasin said the removal of subsidies for the T20 group has no direct link to higher prices of goods and services.
She added that any potential impact would likely be indirect and limited, with no immediate or straightforward pass-through effect on the B40 and M40 groups from this policy change.
The Association of Malaysian Hauliers executive secretary Mohamad Azuan Masud said the association does not view the proposed RON95 subsidy rationalisation for the T20 group as having a direct impact on the haulage industry, as commercial hauliers are more closely tied to diesel-related policies and mechanisms.
HARDEST HIT BY HIGHER FUEL COSTS
Chin said the most affected segments would be last-mile industries including logistics, e-hailing, food delivery and physical distribution services.
"For our logistics and delivery small and medium enterprises, fuel is a variable daily operating expense, not a fixed overhead. Unlike larger corporations, these small traders do not have the cash reserves to absorb a 100 per cent increase in fuel costs for long," he said.
Chin said the pass-through effect is often immediate, with prices in sectors such as food and transport potentially rising within days of a subsidy adjustment as businesses adjust to maintain viability.
Meanwhile, Ida said the transport sector would be the most directly affected, as fuel is a key input cost, although the impact is not confined to the transport industry.
"Nearly all sectors are exposed in some way. For example, even the food industry is affected because goods need to be transported from one location to another, which relies on fuel.
"In terms of cost pass-through, it can happen quite quickly. When fuel or operating costs rise significantly, businesses typically have limited choice and will adjust prices accordingly," she said.
Ida said in many instances, these costs are quickly transferred to consumers, often almost immediately or within a short period of time.
BALANCING SUBSIDY CUTS AND INFLATION RISKS
Chin said the association supports the shift towards targeted subsidies, but said implementation must avoid triggering an "economic shock".
He added that the government should consider targeted support such as fuel assistance or cash grants for highly exposed sectors like small traders and mobile service providers, which typically operate on thin margins.
He said at the same time, stricter anti-profiteering enforcement should be implemented to ensure that any price increases remain reasonable and are not driven by predatory pricing.
"Savings from the rationalisation should be channeled directly back into SME transformation funds, specifically for automation and energy efficiency, to help businesses reduce their long-term fuel dependency," he added.
Chin said given external risks to global oil prices arising from Middle East tensions, the government should remain flexible in adjusting support measures if global volatility increases.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'They were shouting names': First responders recall FRU crash horror
Article:
TELUK INTAN: The memory of the Federal Reserve Unit (FRU) convoy crash remains as heavy and vivid as ever for Fire and Rescue Department personnel who were among the first to arrive at the scene a year ago.
For the rescuers, it was not just another emergency call as it was an episode that would stay with them forever.
What should have been a routine return after duty turned into one of the most devastating road tragedies involving security personnel in recent years.
Senior Fire Officer II Muhammad Fazli Shahril Che Ad, who was among the first responders, recalled arriving at a scene filled with confusion, urgency and grief.
"All the victims at the back of the truck had already been pulled out by their own teammates. But three were still trapped at the front — the driver and two others."
What struck him most, however, was not just the wreckage, but the voices.
"The FRU members were calling out to each other. Some were injured, some had died. They were shouting names, trying to help their friends. It was heartbreaking," he said.
"They refused to give up. Even those who were injured were still trying to help others."
The FRU truck, he said, bore the full force of the impact and the aftermath reflected the violence force of the collision.
For fireman Mohd Khusni Saidin, one image remains impossible to forget —  a trapped officer buried under gravel from the lorry's load.
The victim was identified as Sergeant S. Perumal.
"He was still alive when we reached him. We worked quickly, but his injuries were too severe. Even after we brought him out, he did not survive long. And that stays with you."
Khusni said blood had stained his uniform that day, something he could not  forget.
"We handled many accidents, but this one felt different. They were uniformed personnel, like us. It felt personal," he said.
Meanwhile, Emergency Medical Rescue Services  officer Muhammad Rusyaidi Redzuan said they made two urgent trips between the scene and Teluk Intan Hospital, ferrying critically injured victims.
Inside the ambulance, it was not silence that filled the space, but pain.
"They were in agony. We tried to calm them down."
The emotional weight, he added, was unlike anything he had experienced before.
Looking back, Rusyaidi said what stayed with him most was not just the tragedy itself, but the solidarity shown at the crash
site.
"Their friends were crying at the roadside. Even though we didn't know them personally, we felt it too," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump insists US doesn't need China's help in Iran
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump insisted Tuesday he does not need help from China on ending the war with Iran but said he would talk at length with Xi Jinping when they hold a summit this week.
Trump told reporters before leaving for Beijing that "we're going to have a long talk about it."
China is a top economic partner of Iran, buying much of its oil output, and also an important diplomatic backer.
However, Trump underlined that he does not need Xi to find an exit from the conflict that started two and a half months ago with a massive US-Israeli attack before settling into a stalemate over control of the Strait of Hormuz shipping route.
"We have a lot of things to discuss. I wouldn't say Iran is one of them, to be honest with you, because we have Iran very much under control," Trump said.
"We're either going to make a deal or they are going to be decimated."
Amid reports that China continues to help arm Iran and purchase sanctioned oil, Trump said Xi has "been relatively good."
"They get a lot of their oil from that area. We've had no problem. And he's been a friend of mine. He's been somebody that we get along with."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Second chance at life: FRU crash survivor returns to work
Article:
IPOH:   A year after surviving the Federal Reserve Unit (FRU) crash in Teluk Intan that claimed the lives of nine  colleagues, Sergeant Mohd Parih Ali has finally returned to work.
He described it as a "second chance" at life.
The 45-year-old reported back for duty yesterday, just a day before the first anniversary of  the  incident in Jalan Chikus–Sungai Lampam on May 13, 2025.
The 8.50am tragedy involved a truck, carrying 18 members of the FRU from Unit 5, Sungai Senam Camp here, which was rammed by a gravel-laden lorry that had veered into its lane while the convoy was heading home from Teluk Intan.
Parih said the journey to recovery had been long and painful —  with  three surgeries, nearly a year of medical leave and countless hospital appointments.
"Tomorrow (today) marks exactly one year, and only now am I starting work again.
"I underwent three surgeries and I'm still receiving treatment from orthopaedic and neurology specialists. I still have issues with both knees."
He spent 10 days in Teluk Intan Hospital's Intensive Care Unit (ICU)  after being transferred to Raja Permaisuri Bainun Hospital here for further treatment and rehabilitation.
He said he struggled to sleep during the first two months after the crash due to intense pain and discomfort from wearing a neck brace.
"At night, I would wake up startled. After removing the neck brace and undergoing traditional treatment, only then could I sleep comfortably again."
Parih, who was bedridden for more than a month, started walking again with the help of a cane 10 months after the incident.   Despite the ordeal, he described surviving the tragedy as a blessing.
"People say this is a second chance given by Allah. When I think about the nine friends we lost, maybe God loved them more."
The crash has also left Corporal Mohammad Zulnaidi Muhammad Zulkifli traumatised at the thought of travelling in FRU trucks.
The 35-year-old from Kelantan said he still struggled to get into a truck following the incident.
"Sometimes when I'm alone, I think about my late friends and recite the Al-Fatihah for them."
The accident has left Zulnaidi with eight broken ribs, a fractured hip and a broken jaw.
"One of the broken ribs pierced my liver. My jaw had to be fitted with metal braces for two months because I could not move it at all.
"I could only consume blended food through a straw. There was a time I craved chicken chop and my wife had to blend it. During that period, I lost 10kg," he said.
He was placed on medical leave for 111 days before eventually returning to light duties.
[[nid:1438293]]
Meanwhile, commanding officer of Unit 5, Sungai Senam FRU, Superintendent Kenneth Oliver Samuel, said five of the nine FRU personnel injured in the crash had returned to active duty, while four others were still undergoing medical treatment, including two who were scheduled for surgery.
He said the injured officers had  been steadily recovering.
"Five of them are now involved in daily duties and operations. Four personnel are still undergoing follow-up treatment and two of them will undergo surgery."
He said the FRU had been providing logistical support for those still receiving treatment, including transportation to hospitals and medical centres.
"For follow-up treatment, we will assist. We provide vehicles and personnel to take them to treatment centres.
"This will continue until the end of the year, until surgeries and all related treatment are completed," he said.
On welfare support for the victims' families, including widows, he said government assistance had been fully channelled, with additional support still being provided through various channels.
"We are still in contact with them. We always remind them not to hesitate to reach out if they need help. They are still part of the FRU family."
He added that a committee had been established to investigate the cause of the crash, with the findings already submitted to the relevant authorities for further action.
The accident claimed the lives of Sergeant S.  Perumal, Sergeant Mohd Roslan Abd Rahim, Corporal Mohd Pozli Jaudin, Corporal Nurit Pandak, Corporal Amiruddin Zabri, Corporal Mohamad Hilmi Mohd Azlan, Corporal Muhamad Akmal Muhamad,  Lance Corporal Damarrulan Abdul Latif and Constable Akmal Wafi Annuar.
The injured were Sergeant Mazlan Mat, Sergeant Mohd Parih Ali, Corporal Haslizal Mohd Ali, Corporal Nizam Tarmizi, Corporal Mohd Izwan Ishak, Corporal Mohd Harrisul Ikhsan Mohd Mahabudin, Corporal Syed Isfan Syed Mohammad, Corporal Mohammad Zulnaidi Muhammad Zulkifli and Lance Corporal Muhammad Aniq Taufiq Rozi.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke had said proposals to boost  safety features for vehicles used by security forces following the FRU crash  was presented to the cabinet in  November last year.
He said the ministry was working closely with the Defence and Home Ministries to review the implementation of the proposals.
"This matter  needs to be resolved with the ministries involved. We can provide reports and suggestions, but ultimately the relevant ministries  are the ones who need to take action."
Loke added that implementing the measures would take time as it might  involve retrofitting existing vehicles and acquiring new ones to meet improved safety standards.
On May 16, 2025, the driver of the lorry involved in the accident was charged with nine counts of reckless or dangerous driving causing death.
Rudi Zulkarnian Mat Radi, 45, pleaded not guilty to all the charges, which were framed under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
For each charge, Rudi faces a maximum sentence of 10 years' imprisonment, a fine of between RM20,000 and RM50,000, and disqualification from holding or obtaining a driver's licence for at least five years upon conviction.
The trial is ongoing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tengku Adnan denies knowledge of KL retention pond land transfers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Datuk Seri Tengku Adnan Tengku Mansor has denied any knowledge of   flood retention pond land transfers to developers in the capital  when he was  the federal territories minister.
He said land matters then were not under the  ministry's   purview.
"When I became the minister, land matters had been  given back to the Prime Minister's Department, in which the chief secretary to the government was the chairman of the committee in question.
"I didn't even sit on the board of the land committee. The Land and Mines Department was under the chief secretary to the government," he told the
New Straits Times.
Tengku Adnan's comments came after Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh said yesterday the capacity of flood retention ponds in Jinjang and Batu had shrunk to about 30 per cent of their original 33.5ha after
17 land lots were transferred to developers.
The ownership transfers, she said, had  taken place since 2015.
[[nid:1438351]]
Tengku Adnan was the Federal Territories  minister from 2013 to 2018.
Asked if he knew the identities of the developers, Tengku Adnan replied in the negative.
"I would never allow for retention ponds to be covered up for development. No way.
"Unless the land was given by the Land and Mines Department, on this I wouldn't know.  But was this even the case? It wouldn't be fair to accuse the Land and Mines Department of wrongdoing."
He stressed that he would never allow the function of flood retention ponds to be compromised.
"To cover these retention ponds, these lakes, where we divert water for flood mitigation purposes in Kuala Lumpur, and slowly channel them back into the rivers, I wouldn't do this. It's madness."
Meanwhile, former members of parliament (MP) for Kepong and Batu had called on the government to hold  developers accountable, saying compensation and  remediation must be enforced.
[[nid:1438350]]
Former Batu  MP Chua Tian Chang said the government must initiate an investigation into the approval process to determine if  there had been any wrongdoing.
"If so, the individuals involved, including developers and officials, must face justice," he said.
Chua said he had repeatedly raised the issue.
He said reversing the damage was now "virtually impossible" and instead, called for accountability.
He said  Kuala Lumpur City Hall should identify new areas — including  ponds, parks and forest — to be gazetted as reserves or water retention zones to compensate for the lost capacity.
Former Kepong MP Dr Tan Seng Giaw said those responsible for floods in the city should pay   compensation.
"What they have to do is make sure that those who did the damage pay compensation to the affected people."
He said the obligation to provide alternatives must be written into development contracts.
City Hall and all local authorities, he said, should vet developers' track records before approving new projects.
Malaysian Corruption Watch president Jais Abdul Karim said  enforcement agencies, including the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission,  should investigate the possibility of abuse of power, conflict of interest, procedural breaches and  undue benefit to any parties.
[[nid:1438349]]
He also called for an independent and transparent audit of all land transfers around  flood retention ponds.
"While this matter should not be prematurely labelled as corruption without a thorough investigation, it clearly indicates potential governance weaknesses and regulatory lapses that may have allowed such decisions to be made," he said.
He added that  governance "red flags" must be scrutinised.
"Urban development cannot come at the expense of critical public safety infrastructure.
"If the capacity of flood retention ponds has indeed been significantly reduced, the public has the right to know how this happened and who approved these decisions."
[[nid:1438348]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kremlin says no 'specifics' on ending Ukraine war despite Putin's words
Article:
MOSCOW: The Kremlin said Tuesday there was no concrete plan to end the Ukraine war, days after Russian leader Vladimir Putin suggested Europe's worst conflict since WWII could be winding down.
Moscow and Kyiv resumed attacks overnight after the end of a three-day ceasefire proclaimed by US President Donald Trump, which each side accused the other of violating.
After slamming NATO and wishing his forces a swift advance, Putin at the weekend said that he believed the war was "heading to an end", without elaborating.
Trump echoed this on Tuesday, saying the end of the war was "getting closer" and that he could potentially visit Russia this year.
Putin's words spurred confusion, with talks to end Moscow's offensive so far leading nowhere and the Russian leader showing no sign of backing down over his maximalist demands in Ukraine.
The Kremlin clarified there were "no specifics" about Putin's statement.
"The president said that Russia remains open to contact and that work has been done in a trilateral format," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said.
"The accumulated groundwork in terms of the peace process allows us to say that the end is drawing near... But in this context, it is not possible at the moment to speak about any specifics," Peskov said.
Putin would only agree to meet Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskyy outside of Russia if it were to sign a final peace deal, Peskov added.
The Russian leader made the remarks after a scaled-back Victory Day in Moscow – where nerves were high over the risk of a Ukrainian drone attack – and with Russians increasingly showing signs of war fatigue, hitting Putin's domestic approval ratings.
US CEASEFIRE ENDS
Ukraine said Russia ended the three-day ceasefire by launching more than 200 attack drones that damaged energy facilities and apartment buildings, killing at least four people across the country.
One Russian strike on a residential building killed two people and seriously wounded a baby, who lost a leg, in Kryvyi Rig, Zelenskyy's home city, local authorities said.
"After the end of the three-day partial ceasefire, Russia continues to kill and maim Ukrainians," said Zelenskyy, calling the strike on Kryvyi Rig "cynical."
Moscow's army announced Kyiv had also restarted its retaliatory strikes on Russia, saying its air defence units had downed 71 Ukrainian drones after the ceasefire expired.
Ukrainian drones hit a train station in Russia's western Bryansk region, wounding two railway workers, regional governor Alexander Bogomaz said.
Local officials in the Ukrainian region of Dnipropetrovsk ordered the partial evacuation of families with children from parts of the frontline city of Nikopol.
"Russia must end this war, and it is Russia that must take the step toward a real, lasting ceasefire," Zelenskyy added.
The Kremlin, meanwhile, said it was Kyiv that must give in.
As is usual during the short-term truces throughout the four-year war, both sides accused each other of mass violations.
Negotiations on ending the war have so far led nowhere, largely sidelined by the Iran conflict.
Trump's ceasefire announcement however had raised some hope that US-led talks could be resumed.
Russia's war against Ukraine has led to the deaths of hundreds of thousands of people and forced millions to flee their homes.
Russia has repeatedly urged Ukraine to pull out of areas in the eastern Donbas region it still controls as a prerequisite of any peace deal.
Kyiv has rejected the demand.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysians warn RON95 reform could hit all income groups
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:   Malaysians from all income backgrounds are increasingly worried that removing the RON95 fuel subsidy for the T20 income group could trigger spillover effects, including higher costs for lower- and middle-income households.
Businessman Wins Goh Kok Hoow, 40, from Perak, said subsidy reforms should be evaluated based on their impact on the overall economy rather than income categories alone.
He said rising fuel prices could eventually affect the wider public, especially as the government navigated economic uncertainty.
"The world is facing economic difficulties, with wars and rising costs. The government cannot only think about the T20 or B40 groups, it has to think about the whole country."
He warned that poorly managed subsidy reforms could create broader economic ripple effects.
"If not managed properly, the reforms may not only affect the T20 or B40 groups, but the entire country. The government must consider the national economy."
He said although he belonged to the middle-income group and might be affected, economic stability should remain the priority.
Goh stressed that not all T20 households were wealthy and such generalisations were unhelpful.
A civil servant from Shah Alam, who only wanted to be known as Kamarul, 47, said he did not support the move, warning that the impact would extend beyond the T20 group and affect the whole economic chain.
"It is not just about reducing subsidies. When subsidies are reduced, the T20 group may cut back on spending and that will affect other sectors.
"Those of us at the lower levels will also feel the impact."
He said the issue should not be viewed solely through income classifications, as some T20 households face heavy financial commitments despite earning higher incomes.
"Not all T20 individuals are  wealthy.
"We also need to look at household commitments, such as the number of dependants and other financial responsibilities."
He said many households in the Klang Valley might appear to fall within the T20 category, but still struggled with high living costs and financial burdens.
"In the Klang Valley, many may be classified as T20 on paper, but they still have children, loans and many other commitments.
"If the T20 group reduces spending, businesses will also be affected. Eventually, the impact will trickle down to everyone else."
An accountant, who wanted to be known as  Nabilah, 27, said the subsidy review would likely have a greater impact on the lower end of the T20 group.
She said it would particularly hit those who relied heavily on private vehicles for their daily commutes, whereas those in the higher end of the bracket would not be greatly affected.
"The government should consider the situation carefully.
"Perhaps the government believes the T20 group earns significantly more than lower-income groups."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Not all T20 are wealthy, say earners as RON95 reform looms [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Many Malaysians associate the T20 label with financial comfort, luxury lifestyles and ample disposable income. But for some within the category, the reality is far less cushioned.
For entertainment industry event manager Syakir Nabil, 45, who falls within the Top 20 per cent income bracket, the label feels increasingly detached from his lived reality.
"Honestly, I don't even feel like I belong in the T20 category.
"With three children, ageing parents under my care, a mortgage, car commitments and rising daily expenses, it doesn't feel comfortable at all."
As the government considers removing the RON95 fuel subsidy for higher-income groups, Syakir said the move would further strain households already stretched by fixed obligations.
Based on the 2024 Household Income Survey, the national T20 threshold stands at RM12,680.
Syakir said this benchmark did not reflect actual disposable income, particularly in urban centres.
The price of unsubsidised RON95 remains at RM4.02 per litre, while the rate under the Budi Madani RON95 subsidy programme is RM1.99 per litre.
Syakir said about 80 per cent of his monthly income went to fixed commitments, including housing loans, transport costs, insurance and his children's education.
Unexpected expenses, he said, could quickly push his household into deficit.
Healthcare insurance for his family exceeded RM1,500 a month after repeated premium increases, he said.
"That's just healthcare. We haven't included school expenses, groceries or daily necessities."
He said one of the biggest misconceptions about T20 households was the assumption that higher income equated to financial freedom.
[[nid:1438260]]
"People see the outside only. They think T20 means luxury, but they don't see how difficult it is to sustain that lifestyle when costs keep rising and benefits are reduced."
Syakir said many people in the category spent years building their careers, only to feel increasingly penalised for moving up the income ladder.
"If people work harder to improve their lives, but keep losing benefits, eventually they will ask themselves what the point is."
For global strategy lead Roy Faiz, 38, the issue was less
about affluence and more about financial security.
Roy, who is single and supports his ageing parents, said there was a clear difference between "high income" and genuine wealth.
"A lot of T20 households are asset- and commitment-heavy. On paper, the income may look comfortable, but once you factor in mortgages, family obligations, insurance, healthcare and long-term planning, the pressure remains significant."
He said 65 to 70 per cent of his monthly income goes to fixed commitments, including housing costs, insurance, commuting expenses and emergency savings.
He said many in the T20 group were not beneficiaries of generational wealth, but professionals and entrepreneurs who built their positions over time.
"The biggest misconception is that T20 people live extravagantly with endless disposable income. A lot of us budget carefully. We plan long term and worry about financial shocks like everyone else."
He said any subsidy rationalisation should take disposable income into account rather than only gross household earnings.
"Two households earning the same amount can have completely different realities depending on dependants, parents, medical obligations and commitments."
Roy said removing the fuel subsidy for higher-income groups could have inflationary effects.
"If operating costs rise for businesses and households, prices will increase across the board. The impact will not stop with the T20."
Both men said they were not opposed to reform, but urged policymakers to better understand the realities behind income classifications.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'World faces year of severe fires'
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
THE world could face a year of "particularly severe" wildfires — fuelled by climate change and a potentially strong El Nino weather phenomenon — after a record-breaking start to the year, researchers warned yesterday.
"This year, the global fire season has got off to a very fast start," said Theodore Keeping, a researcher at Imperial College London.
The area scorched by wildfires so far was 50 per cent higher than average for this time of year, he said in a press briefing.
And this is before El Nino has even formed.
El Nino is the warm phase of a natural climate cycle in Pacific Ocean temperatures and trade winds, which influences global weather patterns and increases the likelihood of droughts, heavy rainfall and other climate extremes.
It also adds heat to a planet already warmed from the burning of fossil fuels. The last El Nino helped make 2023 and 2024 the top two hottest years on record.
Some weather agencies forecast the coming event will be even stronger — possibly rivalling a "super" El Nino three decades ago.
"The likelihood of harmful extreme fires could be the highest we've seen in recent history if a strong El Nino does develop," said Keeping, who is part of World Weather Attribution (WWA), a network of climate scientists.
Fires have burnt a total area exceeding 163 million hectares between January and the first week of May, according to data from the Global Wildfire Information System.
The total burnt area was 20 per cent higher than the previous record since global tracking began in 2012, said Keeping.
Records were broken in several countries in West Africa and the Sahel region, as well as Sudan and South Sudan, he added.
The burnt area from Asian wildfires had been nearly 40 per cent larger than the previous record in 2014, Keeping added.
The United States and Australia have also seen unseasonably large areas burnt this year.
WWA co-founder Friederike Otto, a climate science professor at Imperial College London, also warned that "there is a serious risk" that the combination of climate change and El Nino could result in "unprecedented weather extremes" this year.
But she said El Nino was "not the reason to freak out" as it was a natural weather phenomenon that "comes and goes".
"Climate change is the reason to freak out," she said, as "it gets worse and worse and worse".  — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Liew clarifies RON95 remarks after quota cut confusion
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Malaysians can rest assured that the RON95 subsidy quota will remain at 200 litres a month.
Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong said his breakdown of the fuel use pattern at an event was misconstrued as him suggesting that the government's plan was to further reduce the quota to 150 litres.
In March, following the spike in global crude oil prices,  Putrajaya was forced to slash the 300 litres a month quota by 100 litres.
The quota-based subsidy system for RON95 petrol began on Sept 30 last year, driven by a need to fix the long-standing "blanket" policy deemed unsustainable and inequitable.
"Initially in September last year, everyone was given 300 litres per person per month. It has now been reduced to 200 litres. Do you see many people affected?"  Liew said during the Fireside Chat event at Affin Bank here yesterday.
"Really very few. Because the data consistently shows that 80 per cent of Malaysians use less than 200 litres. So, the remaining 20 per cent could adjust by travelling at a different hour, by reducing consumption or by paying a bit more, paying market price.
"The next step is 150 (litres). Sixty per cent of the population use less than 150 litres. And further down, 50 per cent of the population uses less than 100 litres. So, at some point, I think we will have to deal with using Budi95 as a demand management tool."
His remarks sparked support as well as cynical retorts, with some netizens saying such a cap would affect daily commuters, especially middle-income households and those living in areas with limited public transport options.
Many interpreted his remarks as a signal that Putrajaya was ready to reduce the fuel quota.
In a comment on the NST Online Facebook page, user  James Gan wrote: "150 litres is good enough! At least we still get subsidy."
User Siti Mariam said: "There should be proper assessment. The cut should be based on individual consumption rather than average consumption."
User Saiful Hafez Ali said: "Better cap at 100 litres so long as the government does not discriminate (against) T20 people."
User Zezen Raj said: I
sokong
(support)... Eighty to 90 per cent of the (vehicles) on the roads are single passenger only."
After Liew issued a clarification  on his comments, some media organisations amended or removed their earlier reports.
In a Facebook post, Liew said he made the remarks when he was asked about the government's strategy to face the global energy crisis at the event.
"It's unfortunate that what I said was taken out of  context.
"In no instance I announced anything on behalf of the government and in no instance that I announced the 150 litres limit as potential government policy.
"The Budi95 mechanism is a good tool as it provides us with data to understand the travel pattern of Malaysians. The top priority of the government and everyone in the society is to ensure that we have sufficient supply for as long as possible.
"As a society, we must ensure that we have petrol supply for the longest  time and at some point we should build consensus to support the effort to reduce fuel consumption.
"I regret that my comments have caused a media storm and I hope all will consider the full context of what I said."
The episode highlighted the people's sensitivity on the issue of subsidy reforms, particularly for RON95 petrol.
Fuel subsidy rationalisation has been a hot topic as Putrajaya seeks to narrow its fiscal deficit and reduce subsidy leakages.
The controversy over Liew's remarks underscores how policy discussions can be interpreted as imminent decisions.
The government has repeatedly maintained that  85 per cent of Malaysians will continue to receive subsidised fuel under the targeted mechanism.
Higher-income groups and luxury vehicle owners are expected to gradually pay market prices.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim had previously acknowledged the challenges in determining a fair income threshold and usage limit under for the subsidy system.
He said balancing fiscal sustainability with the needs of the middle class remains a key policy challenge, as overly broad subsidy cuts could place pressure on ordinary households while excessively narrow targeting could weaken the reform's effectiveness.
Economists have long argued that targeted subsidies are needed to ensure government assistance reaches lower-income groups while reducing wastage and leakages that benefit higher-income households and foreigners.
Analysts have also warned that communication missteps on sensitive bread-and-butter issues can easily create anxiety among consumers already grappling with cost-of-living pressures.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump 'Golden Dome' missile shield could cost US$1.2 trillion over 20 years
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump's "Golden Dome" missile defence shield could cost a staggering US$1.2 trillion over 20 years, the nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office estimated on Tuesday.
"Of the US$1.2 trillion amount, acquisition costs... would total just over US$1 trillion. That amount includes costs for the system's major components – namely, the interceptor layers and a space-based missile warning and tracking system," CBO said in a report.
"The most expensive component is the space-based interceptor layer, which accounts for about 70 per cent of acquisition costs and 60 per cent of total costs," it said.
Average annual costs for operations and support would meanwhile come in at US$8.3 billion, the report said.
Trump ordered the Pentagon to develop plans for a high-tech missile defense shield in late January 2025, initially dubbing it "Iron Dome for America."
In May 2025, Trump announced that US$25 billion had been earmarked for the project, estimating its total cost at US$175 billion.
However, CBO said the same month that the cost of space-based interceptors to defeat a limited number of intercontinental ballistic missiles could be between US$161 billion and US$542 billion over 20 years.
And the goals for Golden Dome are significantly more expansive, with the 2026 National Defence Strategy saying the Pentagon will "focus on options to cost-effectively defeat large missile barrages and other advanced aerial attacks." - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Don't sabotage new chief commissioner'
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
TAN SRI Azam Baki had a strong message for the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) on his final day heading the agency yesterday: "Don't sabotage the new chief commissioner".
In his farewell address after six years at the helm, Azam, who turned 63 yesterday, urged the commission to fully back the new chief and senior management to ensure administrative continuity and preserve the agency's legacy.
Former High Court judge Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman will take over as MACC chief today.
"Do not sabotage the new chief commissioner because if you do, it means you are sabotaging your own organisation," said Azam during a special appreciation ceremony at the agency's headquarters here.
"Please give your support to the MACC top management because they will continue our legacy. In any organisation, we must remain loyal to the leadership.
"This organisation must remain relevant, as envisioned by the earlier leadership. I continued the legacy they left behind for the sake of the country and the people."
With his departure, Azam officially caps a 42-year career in public service, which began with the former Anti-Corruption Agency before it was renamed MACC.
Reflecting on his four decades in enforcement and public service, he said his career journey was one filled with invaluable experiences and challenges.
He also indicated his intention to continue contributing to society after leaving office.
"I will continue working and serving the people for as long as I have the energy. I will try to contribute based on my experience in promoting good governance and integrity wherever needed."
He paid an emotional tribute to his family, hailing them for silently enduring the pressures as well as their sacrifices.
Fighting back tears, Azam said his family weathered challenges that "they should never have had to face" throughout his time with the agency.
"They went through many hardships and trials because of my responsibilities and duties. I always reminded them not to be overly worried because life is not meant to be feared, but managed wisely," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Big blow to KL flood prevention
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Flood retention ponds in Jinjang and Batu here have lost about 70 per cent of their original capacity after 17 plots around them were transferred to developers since 2015.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh said the original 33.5ha flood mitigation system had been significantly compromised.
She said a technical assessment by the Drainage and Irrigation Department (DID) found that the size shrinkage impaired the ponds' primary flood control function and hampered maintenance work by restricting access to the areas.
"The government cannot continue to allow development that compromises the function of flood retention ponds," she said.
"The interest of the people and the safety of city residents must be prioritised, even if the measures needed require firm decisions to rectify long-inherited problems."
Yeoh said the Federal Territories Department had established a task force chaired by director-general Datuk Azmi Mohd Zain to strengthen coordination and improve flood mitigation efforts in the capital.
She said Kuala Lumpur City Hall would no longer approve planning permission for developments on flood retention pond land until all conditions set by the DID were met.
The move, she added, was aimed at safeguarding the ponds' original function and ensuring maintenance access was preserved amid rising concerns about flood risks in the capital.
The Batu and Jinjang flood retention ponds form part of the larger Kuala Lumpur Flood Mitigation Scheme introduced in the early 2000s to prevent flash floods in the city.
Commissioned by the DID in 2002, the system is designed to divert excess water from Sungai Gombak and Sungai Keroh into the retention ponds during heavy rainfall.
The water would be gradually released back into the rivers once the rain subsides.
According to official records from Kuala Lumpur DID, the ponds in Jinjang, Batu and Kepong were built on former mining land.
The system was designed to prevent the heaviest floods that occur in the capital once every 100 years.
Jinjang is a long-established township in the city's northwest under the Kepong parliamentary constituency, whereas Batu encompasses a wider northern Kuala Lumpur area linked to Sungai Batu and Jalan Ipoh.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ekuinas makes RM7.1b in Bumiputera equity value
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Ekuiti Nasional Bhd (Ekuinas) has generated Bumiputera equity value of RM7.1 billion and total shareholder value of RM8.5 billion since its inception, supported by an investment allocation of RM5.1 billion across 49 portfolio companies.
This performance was backed by cumulative gains of RM5.7 billion, alongside a strengthened strategic portfolio — including the healthcare sector, which increased to 29 per cent of Ekuinas' active portfolio in 2025.
Its proportion of Bumiputera employees in management positions within Ekuinas' portfolio companies rose to 36.9 per cent.
Ekuinas has released its 2025 Impact Report, marking a year of transition and positive progress as the government-linked company strengthened its role within Malaysia's investment ecosystem.
It said the year reflects a more integrated approach to delivering both financial performance and socio-economic outcomes.
It added that private equity remains the core of its strategy, focusing on transforming high-potential companies into market leaders through active ownership.
Ekuinas said that through an initial public offering (IPO) and share transfer exercise, it realised gross proceeds of RM828 million, RM350 million of which were distributed as dividends to Permodalan Nasional Bhd (PNB) for the benefit of its unit trust holders.
"The transaction also marked the first Bumiputera 'Relay Race', with a 60 per cent stake in Orkim Bhd transitioning into long-term institutional ownership under PNB, to support the company's next phase of growth."
Ekuinas said its RM800 million private credit arm has completed its first two transactions, providing syariah-compliant, bespoke financing solutions tailored to underserved mid-market companies.
Its chief executive officer, Aliff Omar Mohamad Omar, said the company's progress in 2025 reflects its continued evolution as an integrated private capital investor, supporting Malaysian mid-market businesses across various stages of growth.
"We remain aligned with national priorities, such as GEAR-UP, while maintaining our discipline in delivering sustainable long-term returns and impact."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Saudi launched secret retaliatory attacks on Iran
Article:
RIYADH: Saudi Arabia launched numerous, unpublicised strikes on Iran in retaliation for attacks carried out in the kingdom during the Middle East war, two Western officials briefed on the matter and two Iranian officials said.
The Saudi attacks, not previously reported, mark the first time that the kingdom is known to have directly carried out military action on Iranian soil and show it is becoming much bolder in defending itself against its main regional rival.
The attacks, launched by the Saudi Air Force, were assessed to have been carried out in late March, the two Western officials said. One said only that they were "tit-for-tat strikes in retaliation for when Saudi (Arabia) was hit."
Reuters was unable to confirm what the specific targets were.
In response to a request for comment, a senior Saudi foreign ministry official did not address directly whether strikes had been carried out.
The Iranian foreign ministry did not respond to a request for comment.
Saudi Arabia, which has a deep military relationship with the United States, has traditionally relied on US military for protection, but the 10-week war has left the kingdom vulnerable to attacks that have pierced the US military umbrella.
GULF ARAB STATES BEGAN HITTING BACK
The Saudi strikes underscore the widening of the conflict — and the extent to which a war that began when the US and Israel launched airstrikes on Iran on Feb 28 has drawn in the broader Middle East in ways that have not been publicly acknowledged.
Since the US and Israeli strikes, Iran has hit all six Gulf Cooperation Council states with missiles and drones, attacking not only US military bases but civilian sites, airports and oil infrastructure, and closed the Strait of Hormuz, disrupting global trade.
The United Arab Emirates also carried out military strikes on Iran, the Wall Street Journal reported on Monday. Together, the Saudi and Emirati actions reveal a conflict whose true shape has remained largely hidden — one in which Gulf monarchies battered by Iranian attacks began hitting back.
But their approach has not been identical. The UAE has taken a more hawkish stance, seeking to extract a cost from Iran and engaging only rarely in public diplomacy with Teheran.
Saudi Arabia has meanwhile sought to prevent the conflict from escalating and has stayed in regular contact with Iran, including via Teheran's ambassador in Riyadh. He did not respond to a request for comment.
The senior Saudi foreign ministry official did not directly address whether a de-escalation agreement had been struck with Iran, but said: "We reaffirm Saudi Arabia's consistent position advocating de-escalation, self-restraint and the reduction of tensions in pursuit of the stability, security and prosperity of the region and its people."
STRIKES, THEN DE-ESCALATION
The Iranian and Western officials said Saudi Arabia made Iran aware of the strikes and this was followed by intensive diplomatic engagement and Saudi threats to retaliate further, which led to an understanding between the two countries to de-escalate.
Ali Vaez, the Iran Project Director at the International Crisis Group, said retaliatory Saudi strikes on Iran, followed by an understanding to de-escalate, would "show pragmatic recognition on both sides that uncontrolled escalation carries unacceptable costs."
